title: Design a Stock Price Alerting / Notification System

transcript.text: Design a stock price alerting system: users set price-threshold alerts on symbols; when a tick crosses a threshold, notify them fast. Millions of alerts against a high-frequency price stream.

blocks.md:
# Design a Stock Price Alerting / Notification System

Users set price-threshold alerts on symbols ("ping me when AAPL crosses $200"); when a tick crosses the threshold, we notify them fast. Millions of alerts, a high-frequency price stream. The core tension: a firehose of price ticks meets millions of standing alert rules, and we have to find the few rules each tick trips — without scanning all of them — then fan out and notify at-least-once without spamming people with duplicates.

---

## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and core entities, draw the high-level design one flow at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — checking in with you as I go. I'll keep cost and ops first-class throughout. Sound good?"

The one-line frame: this is a **streaming match problem dressed as a notification problem** — the hard part isn't sending the push, it's deciding *which* of 50 million standing rules a 5-million-ticks-per-second stream just tripped, in the few milliseconds before the price moves again.

### 🔒 Scope lock (stated as vetoable assumptions — stop me if any is wrong)

1. **The price stream is an external firehose we consume, not produce.** I'll assume we subscribe to a market-data feed (exchange / vendor) that pushes ticks; we don't build the exchange. *If we owned the matching engine, ticks would be internal events — same design downstream.*
2. **Alerts are simple price thresholds: "symbol X crosses price P, up or down."** I'll assume NO complex rules (no "RSI > 70", no multi-leg options spreads) in v1. *This forks the matcher: simple thresholds = a sorted index per symbol; complex rules = a streaming rules engine like Flink with per-rule state.*
3. **An alert fires at most once, then disarms** (one-shot), unless the user re-arms it. I'll assume this over "fire every time it's above" — the latter would spam a user thousands of times in a volatile minute. *This forks dedup and the firing-rate math entirely.*
4. **Latency target is "fast, human-perceptible," seconds not microseconds.** I'll assume p99 from tick-cross to push-handed-to-provider of ~1–2 seconds. *We are NOT building a low-latency trading-execution path where microseconds = money; that forks the whole stack (kernel bypass, colocated, no Kafka hop).*

> 🤝 Here's what's **in scope**: setting/editing/deleting threshold alerts, matching the live stream, one-shot firing, fan-out to push/email/SMS, at-least-once delivery with dedup. **Out of scope** (add or cut before I commit): the trading/execution path, complex technical-indicator rules, charting, portfolio P&L, and historical backtesting. Good?

### Functional requirements (the core flows — "above the line")

Since we assumed simple one-shot thresholds against an external firehose, the core flows are:

**FR1 — Manage alerts (CRUD).** Users create, list, edit, and delete price-threshold alerts on a symbol.
- 🗣️ Plain words: a user opens the app and says "tell me when Tesla drops below $150," and can later change or cancel it.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: these 50M rules have to be organized so the stream can find the relevant ones *instantly* per symbol — the write path quietly determines whether the read/match path is even possible.

**FR2 — Match the price stream against alerts.** For every incoming tick, find the alerts whose threshold the price just crossed.
- 🗣️ Plain words: a price update for Apple comes in, and we figure out which people asked to be told about exactly this price move.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: ~5M ticks/s × 5K alerts/symbol = 25 *billion* comparisons/s if you do it naively. The whole design lives or dies on never doing that.

**FR3 — Fire one-shot and fan out a notification.** When an alert trips, atomically mark it fired (so it won't re-fire) and emit notifications to the user's channels.
- 🗣️ Plain words: once it crosses, send the alert once — not fifty times because the price wobbled around the line.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: the same tick can be processed twice (retries, restarts); we must guarantee a user sees *one* notification per fire, not duplicates.

**FR4 — Deliver across channels (push / email / SMS) at-least-once.** Hand the notification to APNs/FCM/SES/SMS with retries, dedup, and per-user preferences.
- 🗣️ Plain words: actually get the message to the phone, and if a provider blips, retry without double-sending.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: third-party providers fail and are slow; "at-least-once + retries" must not become "the user got it three times."

#### Below the line (out of scope)
Trading / order execution, complex indicator rules (RSI/MACD/spreads), charts & visualization, portfolio tracking, historical replay/backtesting, in-app social, and the market-data feed itself (we consume it).

### Non-Functional requirements (PROVISIONAL targets — tagged to the assumption they lean on)

**NFR1 — Match latency p99 ~1–2s from tick-cross to notification-enqueued** (ASSUMING the "seconds not microseconds" lock; tightens to sub-100ms only if we drop the Kafka hop).
- 🗣️ Plain words: within a second or two of the price crossing, the alert is on its way to your phone.
- 💥 What breaks without it: a user gets "AAPL crossed $200" a minute late, after it's already back at $195 — the alert is worthless and trust evaporates.

**NFR2 — Match correctness: no missed crossings, at-least-once firing** (ASSUMING one-shot semantics).
- 🗣️ Plain words: if the price genuinely crossed your line, you *will* be told — we never silently drop a crossing.
- 💥 What breaks without it: a user set an alert specifically so they wouldn't have to watch the screen; a missed fire is the worst possible failure for this product.

**NFR3 — Dedup: at most one user-visible notification per fire** (ASSUMING at-least-once delivery, so dedup is mandatory).
- 🗣️ Plain words: you get told once, even though our system retries internally.
- 💥 What breaks without it: retries turn one crossing into a stream of identical buzzes; users disable notifications, which kills the product.

**NFR4 — Availability: matcher + ingest 99.95%, notification path 99.9%.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: 99.95% is under ~4.5 hours of downtime per year; the matching pipeline is the part that *must* stay up during market hours.
- 💥 What breaks without it: if the matcher is down for ten minutes during a volatile open, every crossing in that window is lost forever (the stream doesn't replay your missed evaluations unless we buffer it).

**NFR5 — Cost & ops first-class (Amazon lens): bound the per-tick CPU cost and the per-notification cost; alarms on consumer lag.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: processing 5M ticks/s can quietly cost a fortune in compute, so we watch the cost-per-tick, not just the cost-per-alert.
- 💥 What breaks without it: an unbounded matcher fleet scales linearly with the firehose and the AWS bill balloons while almost nothing is firing.

> 💬 "The trick to frame up front: notifications are *rare* — maybe a couple million fires a day — but the *stream* is enormous. So I'll spend my entire engineering budget on the match step and treat fan-out as the easy part."

> 🎙️ Script: "We're building price-threshold alerts. Users CRUD simple one-shot thresholds, we match an external price firehose against them, fire each alert once, and fan out to push, email, or SMS at-least-once with dedup. The targets that matter: a second or two from cross to notification, never miss a real crossing, and exactly one buzz per fire even though we retry internally. I'm explicitly leaving out trading execution and fancy indicator rules — this is alerting, not a trading engine."

---

## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)

These are the residual forks from my §1 scope lock — the assumptions where, if I guessed wrong, the architecture changes shape. I'm not re-asking scale (I've committed to ~50M alerts, ~5M ticks/s); I'm surfacing where the design is sensitive.

**Fork A — I locked "simple price thresholds," not a general rules engine.**
- ↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:
  - If alerts are truly just price thresholds → a **per-symbol sorted index** (an interval tree / Redis sorted set) is enough; matching is an O(log n) probe per tick. This is the design I'll build.
  - If alerts include technical indicators or multi-condition logic → I'd need a **stateful stream processor** (Flink/Kafka-Streams) holding rolling per-symbol windows and per-rule state, which is a fundamentally heavier, more expensive system.
- 🗣️ How I'd say it: "I'm assuming plain price thresholds. If you want RSI-style or multi-leg rules, tell me now — that's a stateful streaming engine, not a sorted index, and it roughly triples the build."

**Fork B — I locked "one-shot firing," not "fire continuously while true."**
- ↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:
  - If one-shot → an alert fires once then disarms; total fires/day is a small fraction of all alerts (~5%), so fan-out is trivial.
  - If "fire every tick it's above the line" → firing rate explodes toward the *tick* rate, dedup becomes "one per minute per user" rate-limiting, and the notification path becomes a real scaling problem in its own right.
- 🗣️ How I'd say it: "I'm treating each alert as fire-once-then-disarm. If you want repeated alerts while a condition holds, the notification volume changes by orders of magnitude and I'd add per-user rate-limiting as a first-class component."

**Fork C — I locked "seconds, not microseconds" latency.**
- ↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:
  - If seconds are fine → I can afford a durable Kafka hop between ingest and matcher (buys me replay and back-pressure for free).
  - If this is a sub-millisecond trading-grade path → I'd drop the broker, run the matcher in-process on the feed handler with shared memory, colocate, and accept losing replay/durability for speed.
- 🗣️ How I'd say it: "I've assumed human-perceptible latency, a second or two. If this is feeding an automated trading strategy where microseconds matter, that's a different machine — colocated, no broker — and I'd design it differently."

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "Those are my three load-bearing assumptions — simple thresholds, one-shot firing, seconds-not-microseconds. Any of those you want to flip before I start sizing and drawing?"

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I ran a small capacity script (`capacity.py`) so the arithmetic is exact; I'll surface only the numbers that drive a decision. Quick glossary: **QPS** = queries (here, ticks or sends) per second; **tick** = one price update for one symbol; **fire** = an alert whose threshold the price just crossed; **fan-out** = turning one fire into per-channel sends.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Live alerts | 5M active users × ~10 alerts each | **~50M** | "about fifty million standing alerts" | Index must be sharded; can't scan |
| Avg alerts / symbol | 50M ÷ 10K symbols | **~5,000** | "around five thousand per symbol" | Per-symbol index, not per-alert |
| Ingest avg | 10K symbols × 100 ticks/s blended | 1.0M/s | "a million ticks a second" | Stream, not request/response |
| **Ingest peak** | 1.0M/s × 5 burst (open + macro prints) | **~5M/s** | "five million ticks a second at the open" | **THE binding bottleneck** |
| Naive match | 5M ticks/s × 5K alerts/symbol | ~25B/s | "twenty-five *billion* comparisons" | Proves we MUST index |
| Indexed match | 5M ticks/s × one O(log n) probe each | **~5M probes/s** | "five million cheap probes a second" | Matcher fleet sized on probes/s |
| Daily fires | 50M alerts × ~5% trip/day | ~2.5M/day | "a couple million fires a day" | Fan-out is *not* the bottleneck |
| **Fire peak** | 2.5M ÷ 23.4K trading-secs × 5 burst | **~530/s** | "a few hundred fires a second" | Notification fleet is tiny |
| Notif peak | ~530/s × 1.5 channels | ~800/s | "under a thousand sends a second" | One small sender fleet covers it |
| **Hot symbol** | 50M × 2% on one ticker | **~1M alerts** | "a million alerts on one ticker" | **Forces sub-sharding a symbol** |
| Hot symbol ticks | 100 × 5 burst × 50× hotness | ~25K/s | "twenty-five thousand ticks/s on one name" | Single partition can't always cope |
| Alert storage | 50M × 200B × 2 idx × 3 repl × 1.5 | **~90 GB** | "under a hundred gigs" | Storage is a non-issue |
| Event log @90d | ~3.4 GB/day × 90 | **~300 GB** | "a few hundred gigs" | Partition-drop retention, no DELETE |
| Cost | matchers + stream + DB + email/SMS | **~$140K/mo** | "low six figures a month" | Cost lives in the matcher fleet |

**The ONE decision-forcing number and its flip threshold.** Two related ones:
1. **Peak ingest ~5M ticks/s** is the bottleneck. The naive "scan all alerts per tick" gives ~25 *billion* comparisons/s — impossible. The indexed approach turns each tick into a single O(log n) probe into that symbol's sorted index, ~5M probes/s, which a sharded fleet handles. **Flip:** if alerts became complex stateful rules, the per-tick cost stops being O(log n) and we'd need a stream processor.
2. **A hot symbol with ~1M alerts.** A single matcher partition tops out around ~50K index ops/s. A megacap ticking ~25K/s is *fine* on one partition — until a fast 2% move crosses a large slice of those 1M alerts in one burst and the crossing-fan-out per tick blows past the ceiling. **Flip threshold:** when one symbol's `tick_rate × in-band-alert-count` exceeds ~50K ops/s, we **sub-shard that symbol by price band** so one ticker spans N matchers.

> 🎙️ Script: "The number that drives everything is five million ticks a second at the open. If I match naively that's twenty-five billion comparisons a second, which is a non-starter — so I index alerts per symbol and turn each tick into one cheap lookup. The flip side surprises people: actual *fires* are only a couple hundred a second, so sending notifications is the easy part. I'll spend my whole budget on the match step and on one nasty case — a single megacap ticker holding a million alerts, which I'll have to split across machines."

---

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first-draft scaffold — full field definitions land in §7. The entities:

**Alert** — one user's standing rule: symbol, threshold price, direction (above/below), state (armed/fired/disabled), channels. It *owns* the firing decision and the one-shot disarm.
- 🗣️ Analogy: like a sticky note on a price — "buzz me when Tesla drops under $150" — that takes itself down once it's done its job.
- ⚠️ Probe: "How do you stop the same alert firing twice when the price wobbles across the line?" → the Alert has an atomic `armed → fired` state transition guarded by a conditional update; only the transition that wins gets to enqueue a notification.

**Symbol (with its alert index)** — a tradable instrument and the *sorted index* of all armed thresholds on it. It *owns* the data structure that makes matching O(log n).
- 🗣️ Analogy: think of each ticker as a ruler with little flags pinned at every price someone cares about; a tick is a finger sliding along the ruler, and we only look at the flags it passes.
- ⚠️ Probe: "What happens to a symbol where everyone watches the same name?" → that's the hot-shard problem; we sub-shard the symbol's index by price band so no single machine owns all million flags.

**PriceTick** — an ephemeral event: symbol, price, timestamp, sequence. It *owns* nothing persistent; it flows through, triggers matching, and is dropped (we don't store every tick).
- 🗣️ Analogy: like a heartbeat — we react to it the instant it arrives, but we don't keep a diary of every beat.
- ⚠️ Probe: "Do you persist ticks?" → no — the firehose is transient; we keep a short Kafka buffer for replay/back-pressure, not a tick database. Storing 5M/s would be the real cost trap.

**NotificationEvent** — the record of a fire and its delivery attempts: alert_id, user, channel, dedup_key, status, provider_id. It *owns* the at-least-once + dedup audit trail.
- 🗣️ Analogy: like a delivery receipt — proof we tried to tell you, what channel, and whether it landed.
- ⚠️ Probe: "How is this the biggest table?" → it's append-only and grows with fires, so it gets a retention policy and partition-drop; everything else is small.

**User (channels + preferences)** — identity, devices/tokens, channel preferences, quiet hours. It *owns* the fan-out target list.
- 🗣️ Analogy: your address book entry — which phone, which email, and "don't buzz me at 3am."

> 🎙️ Script: "Five entities. The Alert is the standing rule and it owns the one-shot fire. The most important and least obvious one is the Symbol's *alert index* — that sorted structure per ticker is the whole reason matching is cheap, and it's where the hot-symbol problem bites. PriceTicks are deliberately throwaway — I do *not* store the firehose. NotificationEvents are the audit trail and the one big table. I'll fill in fields in the data model."

---

## 5. API / Interface

Everything is versioned under `/v1`. List endpoints use **cursor (keyset) pagination** — a `cursor` token over `(created_at, id)` — not offset/limit, because offset gets slower as the list grows and skips or duplicates rows when alerts are added/removed under you. Security: the caller is identified from a **JWT** (a signed token proving who you are); the server never trusts a client-supplied `userId`. Object-level authz on every mutating call: "can *this* caller act on *this* alert?" is checked by matching the alert's owner to the JWT subject.

**POST /v1/alerts — create an alert** (POST because it creates new server state).
- Key fields: `symbol: string` (validated against the tradable universe); `threshold: number`; `direction: enum above|below`; `channels: string[]` (subset of the user's configured channels). Absent on purpose: `userId` (taken from the JWT, never the body) and `status` (server initializes to `armed` — the client can't forge a fired/disabled state).
- One key decision: **idempotency.** Client sends an `Idempotency-Key` header; a retried create returns the *same* alert, never a duplicate rule.

```
// Request
POST /v1/alerts
Idempotency-Key: 7c1f-...-a9
{ "symbol": "AAPL", "threshold": 200.00, "direction": "above", "channels": ["push","email"] }

// Response 201
{ "alertId": "al_8fd2", "symbol": "AAPL", "threshold": 200.00, "direction": "above",
  "status": "armed", "channels": ["push","email"], "createdAt": "2026-06-21T14:30:00Z" }
```

**GET /v1/alerts?status=armed&cursor=... — list my alerts** (GET, read-only). Returns only the caller's alerts (authz from JWT). Cursor paginated.

```
// Response 200
{ "items": [ { "alertId": "al_8fd2", "symbol": "AAPL", "threshold": 200.0, "status": "armed" } ],
  "nextCursor": "eyJjIjoiMjAyNi0wNi0yMSJ9" }
```

**PUT /v1/alerts/{alertId} — edit an alert** (PUT replaces the rule fully). Authz: the alert's owner must equal the JWT subject, else 404 (not 403 — we don't leak existence of others' alerts). Editing a `fired` alert re-arms it.

**DELETE /v1/alerts/{alertId} — cancel** (DELETE removes state). Idempotent by nature: deleting an already-deleted alert returns 204.

**POST /v1/users/me/channels — register a device/channel** (POST, creates a channel binding). Body carries the push token / verified email; server associates it with the JWT subject. The raw push token is stored server-side and never echoed back in full.

```
// Request
POST /v1/users/me/channels
{ "type": "push", "token": "fcm_d39a...", "deviceLabel": "iPhone 15" }

// Response 201
{ "channelId": "ch_a1", "type": "push", "verified": true }
```

There is **no public "fire" or "send" endpoint** — firing is driven entirely by the internal match pipeline off the price stream, never by a client. That's a deliberate security boundary: a client can never trigger someone else's notification.

> 💬 "Notice the price never comes from the client and the fire is never client-triggered — both come from our trusted stream. The client only owns the *rule*, not the *firing*."

> 🎙️ Script: "The API is small and boring on purpose — create, list, edit, delete an alert, plus register your devices. Everything's under slash-v-one, lists are cursor-paginated, and I pull the user from the JWT so nobody can set alerts on someone else's behalf or forge a fired status. The interesting bit is the *absence*: there's no client-facing fire endpoint, because firing only ever comes from our internal match of the trusted price stream."

---

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

Shared color language across all diagrams: blue = clients/external feed, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey = datastores, purple = third-party providers.

### 6.1 "Manage alerts (CRUD)"

A user creates/edits/deletes a threshold rule. The interesting part: a write doesn't just persist a row — it must also update the **per-symbol match index** the stream reads, so a brand-new alert is matchable within milliseconds.

**Architecture decisions**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Alert API service | Stateless HTTP service handling alert CRUD | Simple, horizontally scalable, owns validation + authz | Not a monolith with the matcher — we want to scale match independently of CRUD | One more service to deploy |
| Alert DB (Postgres) | Relational store, source of truth for alert rows | Strong consistency for the one-shot fire transition; relational queries for "my alerts" | Not Dynamo — we need a conditional `armed→fired` update and easy secondary queries | Manual sharding past one node |
| Alert Index (Redis sorted sets) | In-memory sorted structure, per symbol, mapping threshold→alert | O(log n) range probe per tick; rebuildable from Postgres | Not query Postgres per tick (5M/s of point queries kills it); not a custom tree (Redis gives it free) | Index is a derived cache; must be kept in sync |
| Index updater | Consumes alert-change events, updates the sorted index | Decouples CRUD latency from index maintenance | Not synchronous index write in the API (couples write latency, no replay) | A few-ms lag between create and matchable |

> 🎙️ Narration: "The Alert API is a plain stateless service — it validates, checks you own the alert, writes to Postgres. I chose Postgres because the fire transition is a money-grade 'flip exactly once' operation and Postgres gives me a conditional update for that; Dynamo would make that awkward. The clever part is the Alert Index: a Redis sorted set per symbol, where alerts are sorted by threshold price. That's what lets the stream do one logarithmic lookup per tick instead of scanning thousands of rows. I keep it in sync with a small updater that consumes change events, so a new alert is matchable within milliseconds — the trade-off is the index is a derived cache I have to rebuild on failure."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Postgres** — a relational database that guarantees a multi-step write either fully happens or fully rolls back. Here it's the source of truth for alert rows and the one-shot fire flip.
> **Redis sorted set** — an in-memory list kept sorted by a score, with O(log n) range lookups. Here the score is the threshold price, so we can ask "which alerts sit between the old and new price" instantly.
> **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — reading a database's commit log as a stream of change events. Here it (or an outbox) feeds the index updater so the Redis index never drifts from Postgres.

**Create-alert flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Mobile or web client]
    Client -->|"1 POST v1 alerts"| API[Alert API service]
    API -->|"2 authz from JWT"| API
    API -->|"3 insert row"| PG[(Alert DB Postgres)]
    PG -->|"4 change event"| OUT[[Outbox stream]]
    OUT -->|"5 consume"| UPD[Index updater]
    UPD -->|"6 add threshold"| IDX[(Alert Index Redis)]
    API -->|"7 201 armed"| Client
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class API,UPD svc
    class OUT queue
    class PG,IDX db
```
↳ reuses existing: none — this slice introduces the core write path.

**Numbered narration**
1. **Client POSTs the rule.** The API owns CRUD because it's the single validated entry for alert state. After: request in flight. What if malformed symbol → 400 before any write.
2. **Authz from JWT.** The API derives `userId` from the token, never the body. After: caller bound to identity. What if forged token → signature check fails, 401.
3. **Insert into Postgres.** Postgres owns truth because the fire flip needs ACID. After: durable `armed` row exists. What if DB down → 503, client retries with same idempotency key.
4. **Emit change event via outbox.** The row insert and an outbox row are written in the *same* transaction, so we never lose a change. After: change is durably queued.
5. **Index updater consumes.** Decoupled so CRUD latency doesn't wait on Redis. After: event being applied.
6. **Add threshold to the symbol's sorted set.** Now the alert is matchable. What if Redis down → updater retries; index lags but Postgres is still truth, so we rebuild.
7. **Return 201 `armed`.** Server sets status; client can't forge it.

> 💬 "Every alert write fans into two places — the durable Postgres row *and* the in-memory match index — and I keep them consistent with an outbox so the index can always be rebuilt from truth."

> 🎙️ Script: "Creating an alert writes a Postgres row and, through an outbox, updates a per-symbol Redis sorted set so the stream can match it. I split index maintenance into its own consumer so a slow Redis never slows down the user's create call, and because the index is fully rebuildable from Postgres, losing Redis is an availability blip, not data loss."

### 6.2 "Match the price stream against alerts"

This is the heart. A firehose of ticks meets the per-symbol indexes. The interesting part: partition the work by symbol so each matcher owns a slice of symbols and their indexes, and turn each tick into one range probe.

**Architecture decisions**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feed ingestor | Service that connects to the market-data feed and normalizes ticks | Isolates the external protocol; one place to handle reconnects | Not matching inside the feed handler — couples ingest to match scaling | Extra hop |
| Tick stream (Kafka) | Durable log, partitioned BY SYMBOL, buffering ticks | Replay on matcher restart + back-pressure; symbol-partitioning pins a symbol to one matcher | Not direct push to matchers (lose replay, no buffer for bursts) | ~tens of ms added latency |
| Matcher fleet | Stateful workers; each owns a set of symbol partitions and their in-memory indexes | O(log n) probe per tick; in-memory = fast; last-price per symbol held locally | Not stateless lambda per tick (cold index load per call is fatal at 5M/s) | Stateful = needs careful rebalancing |
| Last-price store | Per-symbol last seen price, held in matcher memory + checkpointed | Crossing detection needs old price vs new price | Not re-reading from DB (5M/s reads impossible) | Lose in-memory price on crash → checkpoint recovery |

> 🎙️ Narration: "A feed ingestor connects to the vendor and drops normalized ticks onto Kafka, partitioned by symbol. Kafka buys me two things I really want: replay, so a matcher that restarts re-reads recent ticks instead of missing crossings, and back-pressure, so a burst at the open queues instead of crashing me. Each matcher owns a set of symbols and keeps their alert indexes and last prices in memory — so a tick becomes one logarithmic range probe between the previous price and the new price. I rejected stateless per-tick functions because loading the index on every one of five million ticks a second is hopeless. The trade-off is matchers are stateful and need careful partition rebalancing."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Kafka** — a durable, partitioned append-only log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow or restarts. Here it holds ticks, partitioned by symbol, between ingest and matching.
> **Partition by symbol** — routing all ticks for one ticker to the same consumer. Here it guarantees one matcher owns one symbol's index, so crossing detection is local and lock-free.
> **Crossing detection** — comparing the previous price to the new price and pulling only thresholds in that band. Here it's a sorted-set range query, the reason we touch ~zero alerts on most ticks.

**Match flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Feed[Market data feed]
    Feed -->|"1 raw ticks"| ING[Feed ingestor]
    ING -->|"2 normalized tick"| KAF[[Tick stream Kafka by symbol]]
```

**Numbered narration**
1. **Feed pushes raw ticks.** Ingestor owns the vendor protocol. After: tick received. What if feed disconnects → reconnect + gap detection (NFR2 — never silently miss).
2. **Normalize and publish to Kafka, keyed by symbol.** Keying pins a symbol to one partition→one matcher. After: tick durably buffered.
3. **Matcher consumes its partitions.** It owns those symbols. After: tick being evaluated.
4. **Range probe the symbol's sorted set** between old and new price. Only thresholds in that band are pulled — usually none. After: zero or a few candidates.
5. **Compare against last price** to confirm direction (above vs below crossing). After: confirmed crossings.
6. **Emit fire candidates** to the fire stream. After: handoff to firing. What if matcher crashes mid-batch → Kafka offset not committed, re-consumed on restart (at-least-once, dedup handles dupes downstream).

> 💬 "Most ticks fire *nothing* — the price wiggles inside a band with no pending alert — but I still pay one cheap probe to know that, and that probe is the whole game."

> 🎙️ Script: "Ticks land on Kafka partitioned by symbol, and each matcher owns a slice of symbols with their indexes in memory. For every tick I do a single range lookup between the last price and the new price — almost always it returns nothing, occasionally it returns the alerts that just got crossed. Partitioning by symbol means no locks and the index is always local, which is how we survive five million ticks a second."

### 6.3 "Fire one-shot and fan out"

A crossing must become *exactly one* fire per alert, even though the pipeline is at-least-once. The interesting part: an atomic `armed→fired` flip is the dedup point of truth.

**Architecture decisions**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Firing worker | Consumes fire candidates, flips alert state, enqueues notifications | Conditional update gives exactly-once *effect* on a dupe-prone stream | Not flip inside the matcher (matcher must stay hot/lock-free) | Extra hop |
| Conditional update | Postgres `UPDATE ... WHERE status='armed'` returning rows affected | The DB serializes the flip; only one update wins | Not Redis-only flag (not durable; lose on crash) | A Postgres write per fire (only ~530/s — fine) |
| Notification queue | Durable queue of per-channel send tasks | Decouples firing from slow providers; retries | Not synchronous provider call from firing (blocks on third-party latency) | Async, eventual delivery |

> 🎙️ Narration: "When a candidate arrives, the firing worker does a conditional Postgres update: set status to fired *where it's still armed*. If it affected one row, this worker won the flip and it enqueues the notification; if it affected zero rows, a dupe already fired it and we drop silently. That single conditional write is my dedup point — it turns an at-least-once stream into an exactly-once *user effect*. I rejected doing the flip inside the matcher because the matcher has to stay lock-free and hot. Then I enqueue per-channel sends so firing never blocks on a slow provider."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Conditional update** — a database write that only applies if the row is still in an expected state, returning how many rows changed. Here it's how exactly one fire wins among duplicates.
> **At-least-once → exactly-once effect** — the pipeline may deliver a candidate twice, but the conditional flip ensures only one produces a notification. Here that's the whole dedup story.

**Fire + fan-out flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FIRE[[Fire stream]]
    FIRE -->|"1 fire candidate"| FW[Firing worker]
    FW -->|"2 conditional flip armed to fired"| PG[(Alert DB Postgres)]
    PG -->|"3a 1 row won"| FW
    PG -.->|"3b 0 rows dupe drop"| FW
    FW -->|"4 write event"| EV[(Notification events)]
    FW -->|"5 enqueue per channel"| NQ[[Notification queue]]

    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class FW svc
    class FIRE,NQ queue
    class PG,EV db
```
↳ reuses existing: Fire stream (from §6.2), Alert DB Postgres (from §6.1).

**Numbered narration**
1. **Consume a fire candidate.** After: candidate in worker. What if duplicated → handled at step 2.
2. **Conditional flip `armed→fired`.** Postgres serializes it. After: state durably fired.
3. **Branch on rows affected.** 1 row → we won, proceed; 0 rows → a dupe, drop. This is dedup.
4. **Write a NotificationEvent** (audit + dedup key). After: durable record.
5. **Enqueue per-channel sends.** After: handoff to delivery. What if worker crashes between flip and enqueue → reconciliation job scans `fired` alerts with no event and re-enqueues.

> 💬 "The whole one-shot guarantee collapses to one conditional UPDATE — only the worker that flips armed-to-fired gets to notify; everyone else loses the race and stays quiet."

> 🎙️ Script: "Firing is where I turn at-least-once into one-buzz-per-fire. A worker tries to flip the alert from armed to fired in Postgres; the database guarantees exactly one flip wins, and only the winner enqueues a notification. Duplicates from the stream see zero rows updated and silently drop. Then I write an audit event and hand per-channel sends to a queue so a slow email provider never stalls the pipeline."

### 6.4 "Deliver across channels at-least-once"

Get the message to push/email/SMS with retries and provider-side dedup. Interesting part: at-least-once + retries must not double-deliver to the user.

**Architecture decisions**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Channel senders | Per-channel workers (push/email/SMS) calling providers | Isolate provider quirks + per-channel rate limits | Not one mega-sender (one slow provider blocks all) | More worker types |
| Dedup store (Redis) | Short-TTL set of delivered `dedup_key`s | Suppress double-send on retry within the retry window | Not rely only on DB (extra round-trip per send) | TTL must exceed max retry window |
| Provider idempotency | Pass our `dedup_key` as the provider's idempotency token | Even a duplicate API call collapses provider-side | Not blind resend (double buzz) | Depends on provider support |
| DLQ | Dead-letter queue for sends that exhaust retries | Don't lose; alert on-call; manual/replay | Not infinite retry (poison message storms) | Needs a runbook |

> 🎙️ Narration: "Each channel gets its own sender so a slow SMS gateway can't stall push. Every send carries a dedup key; before sending I check a short-lived Redis set, and I also pass the key as the provider's idempotency token, so even if I do call twice the user gets one message. Sends that keep failing go to a dead-letter queue with an on-call alarm rather than retrying forever and creating a storm. The trade-off is delivery is eventual and dedup depends on a TTL that must outlive my retry window."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Dedup key** — a stable id (alert_id + fire timestamp + channel) attached to a send. Here it's the key both our Redis set and the provider use to collapse duplicates.
> **DLQ (Dead-Letter Queue)** — a queue for messages that failed all retries. Here it catches undeliverable notifications for on-call inspection instead of looping forever.
> **APNs / FCM / SES** — Apple/Google push services and AWS's email service. Here they're the external delivery providers we fan out to.

**Numbered narration**
1. **Consume a send task.** After: task in sender.
2. **Check dedup store.** Seen → drop (idempotent retry). After: known-new or dropped.
3. **Deliver to provider with idempotency token.** Provider collapses dupes too. What if provider 5xx → backoff + retry.
4. **Mark sent** in the event log. After: audit complete.
5. **Exhausted retries → DLQ + alarm.** What if provider down for an hour → DLQ fills, on-call paged, replay when healthy.

> 💬 "At-least-once gets us reliability; the dedup key plus provider idempotency turns it back into one-buzz-per-fire for the human."

> 🎙️ Script: "Delivery is per-channel workers with retries. Every send carries a dedup key checked against a short-TTL Redis set and passed to the provider as an idempotency token, so retries never double-buzz. Failures that exhaust retries land in a dead-letter queue with an alarm — I'd rather page on-call than lose a notification or spam a retry loop."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

### PART A — Entity table

| Entity | Key fields | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Alert | **alertId (PK)**, userId, symbol, threshold num, direction, status, channels, version | Postgres | `symbol` (so a symbol's alerts colocate) | Strong (fire flip) |
| AlertIndex entry | symbol → sorted (threshold, alertId) | Redis sorted set | `symbol` | Derived / eventual from Postgres |
| PriceTick | symbol, price, ts, seq | Kafka (transient) + matcher memory | `symbol` | N/A (ephemeral) |
| NotificationEvent | **eventId (PK)**, alertId, userId, channel, dedupKey, status, providerId, ts | Cassandra (time-partitioned) | `(day, alertId)` | Eventual; idempotent by dedupKey |
| User / Channel | userId, channels[], tokens, prefs, quietHours | Postgres | `userId` | Strong |

### PART B — Storage decision cards

**🗄️ Postgres — used for: Alert, User/Channel**
1. What it is: a relational database where a multi-step write either fully commits or fully rolls back, with row-level locking.
2. Why we picked it: the one-shot fire is a `UPDATE ... WHERE status='armed'` that MUST flip exactly once under concurrent duplicate candidates — only a relational DB's conditional update + row lock guarantees one winner cleanly. Volume is tiny (~530 flips/s).
3. Considered instead: **DynamoDB** — conditional writes exist but the relational "my alerts" queries and the outbox pattern are clumsier and it's vendor lock-in; **MySQL** — equivalent, Postgres preferred for partial indexes; **Redis-only** — not durable, can't be the source of truth for money-grade state.
4. Trade-off: we shard manually past one node (shard by symbol), so cross-symbol queries are rare and fine.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Alerts live in Postgres because firing is a flip-exactly-once operation — I lean on a conditional update so duplicate candidates can't double-fire. Volume's trivial, a few hundred writes a second, so a single primary with replicas is plenty; I shard by symbol only when I outgrow it."

**🗄️ Redis — used for: AlertIndex, Dedup store, Last-price**
1. What it is: an in-memory data store with sorted sets that support O(log n) range queries.
2. Why we picked it: matching needs "give me all thresholds between old and new price" *per tick at 5M/s* — only an in-memory sorted structure does that fast enough; querying Postgres per tick is impossible.
3. Considered instead: **Postgres B-tree index** — disk round-trip per tick at 5M/s saturates it instantly; **a hand-rolled interval tree on disk** — months of work, Redis gives sorted ranges free; **Memcached** — no sorted range queries.
4. Trade-off: the index is a *derived cache* — it can be lost and must be rebuildable from Postgres (it is, via the outbox).
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "The match index is Redis sorted sets per symbol. I need range lookups at firehose speed and that has to be in memory. It's derived, not truth — if a node dies I rebuild it from Postgres, so I get speed without risking data."

**🗄️ Kafka — used for: tick stream, fire stream, notification queue**
1. What it is: a durable, partitioned append-only log that buffers events so a slow or restarting consumer loses nothing.
2. Why we picked it: it gives back-pressure for the open-bell burst and *replay* so a restarted matcher re-reads recent ticks instead of silently missing crossings (NFR2); partition-by-symbol pins a symbol to one matcher.
3. Considered instead: **RabbitMQ / SQS** — fine for the notification queue but weaker at firehose throughput and ordered partitioned replay; **direct gRPC push** — no buffer, a burst drops ticks.
4. Trade-off: an extra hop adds tens of ms — acceptable under our seconds-level SLO.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "The firehose rides Kafka partitioned by symbol. I want two things: a buffer so the open doesn't crush me, and replay so a matcher that restarts re-reads ticks rather than missing a crossing — and missing a crossing is the one failure this product can't have."

**🗄️ Cassandra — used for: NotificationEvent log**
1. What it is: a wide-column store built for high-volume append-only writes, partitioned and replicated, with cheap time-bucketed retention.
2. Why we picked it: it's the biggest, fastest-growing table (append-only audit), write-heavy, and we want time-partitioned drop for retention — Cassandra's sweet spot.
3. Considered instead: **Postgres** — would bloat and need manual partition management at this growth; **S3 only** — fine for cold archive but we want recent events queryable by alertId for support/dedup.
4. Trade-off: eventual consistency on reads — fine, it's an audit log, not the fire decision.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "The notification audit log goes in Cassandra — it's append-only and the biggest table, and Cassandra lets me drop a whole day's partition for retention instead of deleting rows one by one."

### PART C — Per-operation consistency

| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fire flip `armed→fired` | Postgres | Strong (serialized) | One-shot correctness — only one winner |
| Create/edit alert | Postgres | Strong | Source of truth for the rule |
| Index update | Redis | Eventual (ms lag via outbox) | A new alert matchable within ms is fine |
| Read "my alerts" | Postgres replica | Read-your-own-writes via primary-after-write | After you create, you must see it — route the immediate read to primary |
| Tick match | Redis + memory | Best-effort current | Slightly stale last-price recovers via checkpoint |
| Notification write | Cassandra | Eventual, idempotent by dedupKey | Audit; dupes collapse on key |

**Read-your-own-writes:** right after `POST /v1/alerts`, the client's immediate `GET` is routed to the Postgres **primary** (not a lagging replica), and the API returns the created object inline, so the user always sees their new alert even if replicas lag.

### PART D — Data lifecycle (the largest table)

The **NotificationEvent** log grows ~3.4 GB/day; at 90-day retention that caps at ~300 GB (from `capacity.py`). Reclamation is **time-partitioned tables with a partition DROP** — at day 91 we drop the day-1 partition in O(1), instantly reclaiming space, rather than a row-by-row DELETE that churns indexes and never frees space cleanly. Anything that must be kept longer (compliance) is rolled to **S3/Glacier** cold archive as Parquet before the drop. Ticks themselves are *never* persisted beyond Kafka's short retention (hours) — persisting 5M ticks/s would dwarf everything and is the real storage trap to avoid.

> 🎙️ Script: "The core is Postgres because firing is a flip-exactly-once. The match index and dedup set are Redis, because matching needs in-memory sorted lookups at firehose speed and dedup needs a fast TTL set. Kafka carries the firehose with replay so I never miss a crossing on restart. The one big table — the notification audit log — goes to Cassandra with time-partitioned drops for retention. The thing I considered and rejected is storing the ticks themselves: at five million a second that's a money pit, so they're transient by design."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 If you get stuck: go back to the requirements and the bottleneck — 5M ticks/s, never miss a crossing, never double-buzz — and reason forward from there.

### How do we match 5M ticks/s against 50M alerts without scanning?

**#### Bad: scan all alerts per tick**
- 🗣️ Plain words: for every price update, walk through every alert on that symbol and check it.
- Approach: on each tick, loop the symbol's ~5K alerts, compare threshold.
- Why people try this: it's the obvious, correct-looking first cut.
- ⚠️ What breaks: 5M ticks/s × 5K alerts = **~25 *billion* comparisons/s** — no fleet affords that; CPU melts and latency blows past seconds into minutes.
- 🔁 Forces upgrade: any real symbol count makes this impossible on day one.

**#### Good: per-symbol sorted index with crossing range probe**
- ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: it touched *every* alert when almost none are relevant; we should touch only the alerts the price actually *crossed*.
- 🗣️ Plain words: keep each symbol's alert prices sorted like a ruler, and when the price moves from A to B, only look at the flags between A and B.
- Approach: per-symbol **Redis sorted set** (score = threshold). Hold the last price in matcher memory. On a tick, do one range query `(old_price, new_price]` — O(log n + k) where k = alerts actually crossed (usually 0). Partition Kafka by symbol so one matcher owns one symbol's index lock-free.
- Why reasonable: turns billions of comparisons into ~5M cheap probes/s that return nothing on most ticks.
- ⚠️ What breaks: a single matcher partition tops out ~50K index ops/s; a hot megacap can exceed that.
- 🔁 Forces upgrade: one symbol with ~1M alerts and a fast move crosses a huge band in one burst, overrunning a single partition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Tick old to new price]
    T -->|"range query"| SS[(Symbol sorted set)]
    SS -->|"k crossed alerts usually 0"| OUT[Fire candidates]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class T,OUT svc
    class SS db
```

**#### Great: sub-shard hot symbols by price band + consistent-hash the rest**
- ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: it pinned a whole symbol to one partition, so a celebrity ticker's million alerts all live on one overloaded matcher.
- 🗣️ Plain words: for the few wildly popular tickers, split their ruler into price ranges and give each range to a different machine, so no one machine owns all the flags.
- Approach: **consistent hashing** (a scheme that maps symbols to matchers so adding/removing a matcher only reshuffles a small slice) for normal symbols. For symbols flagged hot (alert count or tick rate over threshold), **sub-shard by price band**: e.g. AAPL alerts under $190 on matcher A, $190–210 on B, over $210 on C. A tick is routed to the band(s) it crosses. Hot detection is automatic from per-symbol metrics.
- 🔢 Decision-forcing math: single partition ceiling ~50K ops/s. Hottest symbol ~1M alerts ticking ~25K/s — within ceiling at rest, but a 2% gap-move crossing ~20K of those alerts in a burst spikes well past 50K ops/s → split into ~3–4 bands keeps each under ceiling.
- ✅ Failure matrix:

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Matcher crashes | Kafka offset not committed → partitions reassigned, recent ticks replayed, no missed crossing |
| Hot symbol spikes | Auto-detector raises band count; rebalancer splits index across matchers |
| Index node lost | Rebuild sorted set from Postgres outbox; matcher pauses that symbol briefly |
| Rebalance mid-burst | Brief double-ownership → dupes downstream, collapsed by the fire flip |
| Last-price lost on crash | Restore from checkpoint + Kafka replay re-establishes price |

```mermaid
    CH --> M1[Matcher 1]
    CH --> M2[Matcher 2]

    BR -->|"under 190"| HA[Matcher hot A]
```

> 🎙️ Script: "I never scan. Each symbol's alerts live in a sorted set keyed by threshold, and a tick becomes a single range query between the last price and the new price — on most ticks it returns nothing. I partition by symbol so each matcher owns its indexes lock-free. The one nasty case is a megacap holding a million alerts; a sharp move can cross tens of thousands at once and overrun a single machine, so I sub-shard that symbol by price band across several matchers and route ticks to the band they cross."
> 🧠 If they ask "why not just throw more matchers at it?": "More matchers help the *aggregate* firehose, but one symbol is pinned to one partition — adding machines doesn't relieve a single hot symbol unless I split that symbol itself, which is why I sub-shard by price band."

### How do we guarantee one-shot firing and no duplicate notifications?

**#### Bad: fire whenever the matcher sees a crossing**
- 🗣️ Plain words: the moment a tick crosses, just send.
- ⚠️ What breaks: retries, Kafka redelivery, and rebalances reprocess the same tick → the user gets buzzed repeatedly for one crossing; and a wobbling price re-crosses the line dozens of times a minute.
- 🔁 Forces upgrade: at-least-once delivery makes duplicates a certainty, not a maybe.

**#### Good: atomic armed→fired flip as the dedup gate**
- ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: it had no single point that decides "this fire already happened."
- 🗣️ Plain words: make firing flip a switch that can only be flipped once — whoever flips it sends, everyone else stays quiet.
- Approach: firing worker does `UPDATE alerts SET status='fired' WHERE alertId=? AND status='armed'`. Rows-affected = 1 → win, enqueue notification; = 0 → a duplicate already fired, drop. The DB serializes concurrent flips.
- ⚠️ What breaks: worker crashes *after* the flip but *before* enqueueing → alert is fired with no notification (a lost buzz, the worst failure for NFR2).
- 🔁 Forces upgrade: need a way to recover the gap between "flipped" and "enqueued."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C[Fire candidate] --> U[Conditional update armed to fired]
    U -->|"1 row won"| E[Enqueue notification]
    U -->|"0 rows dupe"| D[Drop silently]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class C,U,E,D svc
```

**#### Great: outbox + dedup key + reconciliation**
- ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: a crash between flip and enqueue silently loses a notification.
- 🗣️ Plain words: write "I fired and I owe you a notification" in the *same* breath as the flip, so a crash can't lose it, and stamp every send with a one-time key.
- Approach: the conditional flip AND a notification-outbox row commit in **one Postgres transaction** (transactional outbox). A relay drains the outbox to the notification queue. Each send carries `dedupKey = alertId + fireSeq + channel`, checked against a TTL Redis set and passed as the provider's idempotency token. A **reconciliation job** periodically scans `fired` alerts whose outbox/event is missing and re-enqueues.
- 🔢 Decision-forcing math: ~530 flips/s peak × one tiny transaction each is trivial for Postgres (well under a single primary's ~thousands of writes/s).
- ✅ Failure matrix:

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Duplicate candidate | 0 rows updated → dropped |
| Crash after flip, before outbox | Same transaction → impossible (atomic) |
| Crash after outbox, before relay | Relay re-reads outbox on restart → delivered |
| Provider called twice | dedupKey + provider idempotency → one message |
| Provider down | retries → DLQ + alarm, replay on recovery |
| Alert re-armed after fire | new fireSeq → new dedupKey, legitimate new buzz |


> 🎙️ Script: "One-shot is a single conditional update — flip armed to fired, and only the one update that affects a row gets to notify. To survive a crash right after the flip, I commit the flip and a notification-outbox row in the same transaction, then a relay drains the outbox to the queue, so the buzz can't be lost. Every send carries a dedup key I check in Redis and hand to the provider as an idempotency token, so retries never double-buzz. A reconciliation sweep catches anything that slipped."
> 🧠 If they ask "why not exactly-once delivery end to end?": "True exactly-once across third-party providers is a myth — I can't make APNs promise it. So I do at-least-once plus idempotent dedup, which gives the user an exactly-once *experience*, which is what actually matters."

### How do we never miss a crossing during ingest/matcher failures?

**#### Good: Kafka replay + gap detection**
- 🗣️ Plain words: keep a short recording of the firehose so a machine that blinks can rewind, and notice if the feed itself skipped.
- Approach: ticks are durable in Kafka for a few hours; matchers commit offsets only after processing. On restart, re-consume from last committed offset. The feed ingestor checks tick **sequence numbers** and, on a gap, requests a snapshot/replay from the vendor.
- ⚠️ What breaks: if the vendor feed itself drops and can't replay, we have a true hole.
- 🔁 Forces upgrade: critical alerts may need redundancy.

**#### Great: dual-feed reconciliation + last-price snapshot**
- ↩️ Previous got wrong: a single vendor feed is a single point of missed crossings.
- 🗣️ Plain words: subscribe to two independent feeds and trust whichever is ahead, and snapshot the current price so a restart re-anchors instantly.
- Approach: two vendor feeds into the ingestor; dedup ticks by `(symbol, seq)`; if one feed gaps, the other fills it. Matchers periodically checkpoint last-price per symbol so a cold start uses snapshot + short replay rather than waiting for the next tick to learn the price.
- ✅ Failure matrix:

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Matcher restart | Replay from offset + price snapshot → no missed crossing |
| One vendor feed gaps | Second feed fills via seq dedup |
| Both feeds gap | Gap alarm; on recovery, snapshot reconciliation re-evaluates crossed thresholds |
| Kafka unavailable | Ingestor buffers to local disk, back-pressures feed |

> 🎙️ Script: "Missing a real crossing is the one failure this product can't have, so the firehose is durable in Kafka and matchers only commit offsets after processing — a restart rewinds and reprocesses. I run two independent vendor feeds and dedup by sequence number so one feed gapping doesn't lose ticks, and I snapshot last-prices so a cold matcher re-anchors instantly instead of waiting for the next tick."
> 🧠 If they ask "isn't a second feed expensive?": "It is, so I'd only dual-feed the symbols people actually have alerts on, not the whole universe — most of the 10K symbols have few alerts."

### How do we apply back-pressure when the open-bell burst hits?

**#### Great: bounded queues + load-shed by priority**
- 🗣️ Plain words: when too much comes in at once, slow down gracefully and protect the important work instead of falling over.
- Approach: Kafka is the bounded buffer; matchers consume as fast as they can and **consumer lag** is the trigger signal. Under sustained lag we (1) autoscale matchers, (2) if still behind, **coarsen** matching for low-alert symbols (batch ticks, evaluate min/max of a window instead of every tick — safe because we only care about crossings, not every price), (3) never drop a tick for a symbol that has alerts. The notification path can shed non-critical channels (email before push) but never the fire flip.
- 🔢 Trigger: consumer lag > N seconds or p99 match latency > 2s → autoscale + coarsen.

> 🎙️ Script: "The open is a five-x burst, so Kafka is my shock absorber and consumer lag is my early-warning signal. If matchers fall behind I autoscale, and if I'm still behind I coarsen — for symbols with no alerts I can batch ticks and just track the window's high and low, because all I ever care about is whether a threshold got crossed, not every wiggle. I never drop ticks for symbols people actually watch, and I never shed the fire flip."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

### 9A — Availability targets
- **Match + ingest path: 99.95%** (~4.5 hours/year down). This is the part that must stay up during market hours — a crossing missed here is gone forever. Mechanisms: **multi-AZ** (the matcher/Kafka/Postgres run across multiple data centres in one region so one building's power loss fails over automatically), dual vendor feeds, Kafka replay.
- **Notification path: 99.9%** (~8.7 hours/year). Slightly looser — a delayed buzz is recoverable via the queue and reconciliation; deliveries catch up.

### 9B — Per-dependency failure table

| Component | What breaks | Graceful degradation — what the USER sees | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vendor feed | No ticks | "Alerts continue working" via second feed; if both down, alerts pause silently | Gap detection + snapshot reconciliation on recovery |
| Kafka | Ticks can't buffer | Ingestor buffers to local disk, back-pressures feed; matching continues from disk | Kafka multi-broker; offsets resume |
| Matcher | A symbol slice stops matching | Brief pause for those symbols; crossings replayed on reassignment | Partition reassignment + offset replay |
| Alert Index Redis | Match probes fail | Matcher pauses affected symbols (does NOT silently pass) | Rebuild sorted sets from Postgres outbox |
| Postgres | Can't flip/CRUD | Alert creates 503; fires queue and flip retries | Multi-AZ failover to replica |
| Notification queue | Sends stall | Buzz delayed, not lost; reconciliation re-enqueues | Durable queue drains on recovery |
| Provider (APNs/SES) | Channel undeliverable | User misses that channel; other channels still fire | Retry + DLQ + alarm; replay |

### 9C — RPO / RTO
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we can lose if it all crashes now. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until service is back.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Alert rules (Postgres) | ~0 | minutes | Synchronous replica + multi-AZ failover |
| Fire state (armed/fired) | ~0 | minutes | Same Postgres txn as outbox |
| Alert index (Redis) | rebuildable | seconds–minutes | Rebuild from Postgres outbox |
| In-flight ticks (Kafka) | minutes (replay window) | seconds | Offset replay; dual feed fills gaps |
| Notification log (Cassandra) | seconds | minutes | Replicated; eventual |

### 9D — Cost
From `capacity.py`, ~**$140K/mo**. Top 3 drivers:
1. **Matcher fleet + Kafka (the firehose)** — ~$120K/mo, the dominant cost. Five million ticks/s is constant compute; this is where the money goes, NOT the database or storage.
2. **SMS** — ~$20K/mo. SMS is ~$0.0075/message; even a couple million fires a month with a 5% SMS share adds up — by far the priciest *channel*.
3. **Email/SES** — ~$1.5K/mo, negligible.
- **Unit economics:** ~$1,800 per 1M *delivered notifications* — but that number is misleading and I'd flag it: cost is driven by *processing ticks*, not by *sending notifications* (notifications are rare). The honest unit metric is **cost per million ticks processed**: from `capacity.py`, the ~$120K/mo matcher+Kafka infra chews ~491B ticks/mo (1.0M/s sustained × 6.5h × 21 trading days), so **~$0.25 per 1M ticks of compute** (~$244 per billion).
- **Biggest optimization:** push the "symbols with zero alerts" filter as early as possible — only ~the symbols people actually watch need full per-tick matching; the rest can be coarsely sampled, cutting matcher compute substantially.

> 🎙️ Script: "When the vendor feed drops, a user's alerts keep working off the second feed; if both drop, alerts pause but we never silently say 'no crossing' — Redis failure pauses a symbol rather than passing it. The expensive part isn't the database or storage, it's chewing through five million ticks a second — so my biggest lever is *not matching symbols nobody has alerts on*. SMS is the priciest channel, so I'd nudge users toward push."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: per-symbol sorted index in Redis vs a stateful stream processor (Flink)**
1. Chose: Redis sorted sets per symbol — cheapest path for *simple thresholds*.
2. Gave up: the ability to evaluate complex stateful rules (indicators, windows).
3. 🗣️ Plain words: like using a sorted ruler instead of a full programmable rules engine — perfect while the rules are just "above/below a price."
4. When this reverses: if we add technical-indicator or multi-condition alerts (Fork A), I switch to Flink with per-rule state.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "I went with a sorted index because the rules are simple price thresholds; the day we add RSI-style rules, I'd move to a stream processor."

**🔀 Decision: durable Kafka hop vs in-process matching for lower latency**
1. Chose: Kafka between ingest and match.
2. Gave up: tens of milliseconds of latency.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: a recording booth between the firehose and the matcher — costs a moment, but nothing's ever lost.
4. When this reverses: if this became a microsecond trading-grade path (Fork C), I'd drop the broker and match in-process.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "I kept Kafka because replay means I never miss a crossing on a restart; if microseconds mattered I'd cut it out."

**🔀 Decision: at-least-once + dedup vs chasing exactly-once delivery**
1. Chose: at-least-once with a dedup key + conditional flip.
2. Gave up: a theoretical exactly-once guarantee.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: I can't make Apple's push service promise once-and-only-once, so I make duplicates harmless instead.
4. When this reverses: never for third-party channels; only if delivery were fully in-house could I attempt true exactly-once.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Exactly-once across third parties is a myth, so I do at-least-once and make dupes idempotent — the user's experience is exactly-once, which is what counts."

**CAP/PACELC framing:** The fire flip is **CP** — under a partition we'd rather fail the flip and retry than risk a double-fire. The match/index path is **AP** — it favours availability and accepts a few ms of index staleness. PACELC: else (normal operation) the match path favours **latency** over consistency (eventual index), while the fire path favours **consistency**.

> 🎙️ Script: "My least-certain calls: a sorted index instead of a full rules engine — right for simple thresholds, wrong the day rules get complex; keeping Kafka for replay at a small latency cost; and accepting at-least-once delivery because exactly-once across third parties isn't real. The firing path is CP — I'll fail-and-retry rather than double-fire — while matching is AP and tolerates a hair of staleness."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "A tick crosses a million alerts on one symbol at once — what happens?"**
Mechanism: that symbol is sub-sharded by price band across several matchers, so the crossed alerts are split; each matcher emits its band's candidates to the fire stream, and firing/fan-out (only ~hundreds/s globally) absorbs the spike via the queue.
🗣️ In plain words: we split the popular ticker across machines so no single one has to handle all million at once.
💬 One-liner: "One hot symbol is split by price band across matchers — that's exactly the sub-sharding I built for the megacap case."

**❓ "How do you make sure you never miss a crossing?"**
Mechanism: ticks are durable in Kafka, matchers commit offsets only after processing, and I run two vendor feeds deduped by sequence number; a restart replays, a feed gap is filled by the other.
🗣️ In plain words: the firehose is recorded, so a machine that blinks rewinds, and I have a backup feed.
💬 One-liner: "Durable Kafka replay plus a dual feed — a crossing can't slip through a restart or a single feed gap."

**❓ "Can the same alert fire twice?"**
Mechanism: firing is a conditional `armed→fired` update; only the one that affects a row notifies, the rest see zero rows and drop.
🗣️ In plain words: firing flips a one-time switch — whoever flips it sends, everyone else goes quiet.
💬 One-liner: "No — the flip is a single conditional update, so exactly one fire wins."

**❓ "Can a user get the same notification twice?"**
Mechanism: every send carries a dedup key checked in a TTL Redis set and passed to the provider as an idempotency token; retries collapse.
🗣️ In plain words: each buzz has a one-time stamp, so retrying never double-buzzes.
💬 One-liner: "At-least-once delivery, but the dedup key makes the user's experience exactly-once."

**❓ "What if the matcher crashes right after flipping fired but before sending?"**
Mechanism: the flip and a notification-outbox row commit in the *same* Postgres transaction; a relay drains the outbox after restart, so the buzz is never lost.
🗣️ In plain words: I write "fired, owe you a buzz" in the same breath as the flip, so a crash can't lose it.
💬 One-liner: "Transactional outbox — the fire and the obligation to notify are atomic."

**❓ "What if the price feed goes down?"**
Mechanism: a second independent vendor feed fills in; if both drop, a gap alarm fires and on recovery we reconcile crossed thresholds from a price snapshot.
🗣️ In plain words: we have a backup feed, and if both die we alarm and catch up afterward.
💬 One-liner: "Dual feed normally; both-down is an alarm plus snapshot reconciliation, never a silent hole."

**❓ "What if Redis (the index) crashes?"**
Mechanism: the index is a derived cache; the affected symbols pause matching (never silently pass) and the sorted sets rebuild from Postgres via the outbox.
🗣️ In plain words: the index is rebuildable from the real database, so we pause briefly and reconstruct.
💬 One-liner: "Redis is a cache, not truth — I rebuild it from Postgres and pause those symbols meanwhile."

**❓ "How do you handle the market-open burst?"**
Mechanism: Kafka is a bounded buffer, consumer lag triggers autoscaling, and under sustained lag I coarsen matching for zero-alert symbols (track window high/low) while never dropping ticks for watched symbols.
🗣️ In plain words: the buffer absorbs the spike, I add machines, and I cut corners only on symbols nobody is watching.
💬 One-liner: "Kafka absorbs the five-x burst; I autoscale on lag and coarsen only symbols with no alerts."

**❓ "How do you stop someone setting alerts on another user's account or forging a fire?"**
Mechanism: the caller is taken from the JWT, never the body; every mutating call checks the alert's owner equals the JWT subject; there is no client-facing fire endpoint at all.
🗣️ In plain words: we trust the login token, not the request, and clients can't trigger notifications.
💬 One-liner: "Identity from the JWT, object-level authz on every write, and firing only ever comes from our trusted stream."

**❓ "Can a user manipulate the threshold price to game the system?"**
Mechanism: the threshold is the user's own choice (that's the feature), but the *current price* comes only from our trusted feed — the client never supplies a price, so they can't fake a crossing.
🗣️ In plain words: you pick your line, but you can't fake the market price that crosses it.
💬 One-liner: "The price is always ours, from the feed — the client can't inject one."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?"**
Mechanism: the matcher fleet + Kafka processing 5M ticks/s dominates (~$120K/mo); the biggest cut is not matching symbols with zero alerts — coarsely sample them.
🗣️ In plain words: chewing the firehose costs the most; skip the work for tickers nobody is watching.
💬 One-liner: "It's the firehose compute, not the DB — and the lever is ignoring symbols with no alerts."

**❓ "Why don't you store the price ticks?"**
Mechanism: at 5M/s, persisting ticks would dwarf all other storage and cost; they're transient — Kafka keeps a few hours for replay, then they're gone.
🗣️ In plain words: storing every heartbeat would bankrupt us and we don't need it.
💬 One-liner: "Ticks are throwaway — a few hours in Kafka for replay, never a tick database."

**❓ "How would you add complex rules like 'RSI over 70'?"**
Mechanism: that's Fork A — swap the sorted-index matcher for a stateful stream processor (Flink) holding rolling per-symbol windows and per-rule state, keyed the same way by symbol.
🗣️ In plain words: I'd move from a sorted ruler to a small programmable engine that remembers recent history per symbol.
💬 One-liner: "Indicator rules need state, so I'd move that path to a stream processor like Flink — same symbol partitioning."

**❓ "How would you support quiet hours or per-user rate limits?"**
Mechanism: the firing worker reads user preferences before enqueueing; quiet-hours fires are held and delivered at window end, and a per-user token bucket caps buzz frequency.
🗣️ In plain words: we respect "don't buzz me at night" by holding the notification, and cap how often any one person gets pinged.
💬 One-liner: "Preferences gate the enqueue step — quiet hours defer, a token bucket throttles."

**❓ "How would you make it multi-region?"**
Mechanism: matching is regional (each region consumes the feed), but alert rules are a single-write-region Postgres replicated out; the fire flip stays in the home region to avoid double-fire across regions.
🗣️ In plain words: each region watches the market, but the 'has it fired' decision lives in one place so two regions can't both buzz you.
💬 One-liner: "Match per region for latency, but keep the one-shot flip single-region so we never double-fire across regions."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a system that watches a firehose of stock prices and pings users when a price crosses a line they set. The design splits into two halves. The read half — really a *match* half — consumes about five million ticks a second off Kafka partitioned by symbol, and each matcher keeps that symbol's alert thresholds in a sorted Redis index, so every tick is one cheap range lookup that almost always finds nothing; the one hard case, a megacap holding a million alerts, I split by price band across machines. The write half is alerts and firing: a user's rule lives in Postgres, and when a crossing happens the hardest problem is firing exactly once on an at-least-once pipeline — I solve it with a conditional armed-to-fired flip committed in the same transaction as a notification outbox, plus a dedup key the provider also honors. The two halves connect through the alert index — writes fill it, the firehose reads it. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: the stream is huge but the fires are rare, so spend everything on cheap matching and make duplicates harmless rather than impossible."
